questions.question: Why do frontend projects benefit from a workspace-specific setup?
options: It makes JavaScript run faster automatically
It prevents every project from needing its own Git repository
It keeps editor, runtime, and package settings consistent per project
It removes the need for npm and pnpm
questions.explanation: A workspace-specific setup keeps formatting, linting, runtime versions, and tool settings consistent for that project instead of leaking across unrelated work.

questions.question: What is the main job of a shell alias?
options: Encrypt environment variables
Shorten or customize frequently used commands
Compile TypeScript to JavaScript
Replace Git branches with tags
questions.explanation: Aliases let you turn long or repetitive commands into shorter ones, which reduces friction in daily frontend work.

questions.question: Which extension is most directly used for code formatting in frontend projects?
options: Prettier
Tailwind CSS IntelliSense
GitLens
Live Server
questions.explanation: Prettier handles automatic formatting and is the common baseline for consistent frontend code style.

questions.question: Why are Node version managers like fnm or nvm useful?
options: They automatically convert CSS into JavaScript
They keep different projects on different Node.js versions
They replace Git with a faster protocol
They generate production databases
questions.explanation: Version managers let you pin the Node version per project so one app does not break another.

questions.question: What is the most useful reason to set editor options in .vscode/settings.json?
options: It makes the repository private
It ensures everyone on the team gets the same workspace behavior
It removes the need for package.json
It turns JavaScript into TypeScript
questions.explanation: Workspace settings keep formatting, indentation, and language tooling aligned for everyone who opens the project.
